title: practical english usage - Part 140
content:
My work is no business of yours.

Have you heard this new idea of the boss's?

Hes a cousin of the Queen's. She's a friend of my father's.

That policeman is a friend of mine.   How's that brother of yours?

determiner + noun + of + possessive or that-Anna^sfriend. Instead, we  use a structure with of + possessive.

We   can say my friend, Anna's friend, a friend or that friend, but not a my friend We   cannot usually put a possessive between another determiner and a noun.

177 a  friend       of   mine,      etc For the older English form thine. ►    318.10.

I've had my breakfast, and the dog's had its breakfast too. (     ... and the Its is not normally used without a following noun.

It's nice to have a room of one's own. (     ... of one's/) One's cannot be used without a following noun; instead, we  use one's own.

Can I borrow your keys? I can't find mine, (     L-ean^find ike-mine.) We do not use articles with mine, etc.

Whose car is that? Whose is that car?

Whose can be used with or without a following noun (►   143,  628).

- Which is your car?

That coat is mine.

- That's my coat.

but they are not determiners, and are used without following nouns. Compare: can express various ideas besides possession. They  are similar to my, your. etc.

Like all 'possessive' words and structures, mine, yours, his, hers, ours and theirs 176 possessive        pronouns:      mine     1 yours,      etc country they may lose this when they get a British Passport.

Dual nationality: if the child possesses the nationality or citizenship of another English. I lore Is an example from an old British passport application form: most common in an informal style, but can also be found in formal written This use of they/them/their has existed for centuries, and is perfectly correct It is 2 correctness I had a friend in Paris, and they disappeared for a month.

They/them/their is sometimes used for a definite person who  is not identified.

sack of potatoes.

No girl should have to wear school uniform, because it makes them look like a them.

I swear more when I'm talking to a boy, because I'm not afraid of shocking Two   examples from interviews: However, they/them/their can also be used when the person's sex is known.

grammar •  178   reflexive pronouns: myself, etc but (for) and except (for).

Reflexives are sometimes used instead of personal pronouns after as, like, 6 reflexives       used    instead      of  personal       pronouns I'll go and see the President himself if I have to.

The house itself is nice, but the garden's very small.

It's quicker if you do it yourself.  The manageress spoke to me herself.

nothing else*.

We can use reflexives as emphasisers, to mean 'that person/thing and nobody/ 5 emphatic       use:  Do   it  yourself.

Close the door after you. He was pulling a small cart behind him.

She's very pleased with herself. (She could be pleased with somebody else.) with somebody else.) not She took her dog with her. (     . . .-with-herseff She could hardly take her dog meaning is clear without using a reflexive. Compare: After prepositions of place, we  often use a personal pronoun (me, you, etc) if the 4 after    prepositions:        She    took     her    dog     with     her.

Jack says that James talks to him  a lot. (James talks to Jack, not to James.) Jack says that James talks to himself a lot. (James talks to James, not to Jack.) A  reflexive only refers to a noun in the same clause. Compare: 3 same    clause I love you for yourself, not for your money.

I'm going to tell her a few facts about herself.

His conversations are all about himself.

or objects.

Reflexive pronouns can refer back not only to subjects, but also to possessives I need to get myself some new shoes.

We got out of the water and dried ourselves, (     .. .-dried-us?) not I cut myself shaving this morning, (     I eut me-...) same person(s) or thing(s) as the subject.

Reflexive pronouns are used as objects, in cases when the object refers to the 2 use:  I cut    myself       shaving.

yourselves, themselves.

Reflexive pronouns are myself yourself himself herself itself oneself ourselves, 1  What    are   reflexive       pronouns?
